ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried noodle dish class; no specific beef-and-egg fried noodle entry exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles, stir-fried, with meat and egg
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated beef-and-egg fried noodle code. A0CKP (stir-fried noodle dish with meat) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for beef and egg fried noodles; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beef and egg fried noodles weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-person hawker or restaurant serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo Database); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for component-level cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exactly matches 'Beef and Egg Fried Noodles'. Values are adapted from the HPB Foodo entry for stir-fried yellow noodles with meat and egg, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked beef (NDB 13365), scrambled egg (NDB 01133), and cooked wheat noodles (NDB 20110). The input per_100g values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 18.89 g) are consistent with this adapted composite and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are not reported in the HPB Foodo composite for this dish category and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for stir-fried noodles
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef and egg fried noodles. GI of 50 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for stir-fried yellow wheat noodles (GI ~50, medium category), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 18.89 g/100g × 450 g serving / 100) / 100 = (50 × 85.0 g) / 100 = 42.5 g GL per 450 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, use low-sodium soy sauce alternatives, and limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal to keep sodium intake below 800 mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate load of 18.89 g per 100 g from yellow wheat noodles with only 0.89 g dietary fibre per 100 g, yielding a medium GI of approximately 50 and a high glycemic load of 42.5 per standard 450 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 150–200 g, substitute yellow noodles with shirataki or high-fibre noodles where possible, and increase the proportion of bean sprouts and vegetables to lower the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.22 g per 100 g and cholesterol at 44.44 mg per 100 g from beef and egg, combined with high sodium at 400 mg per 100 g, present a composite cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request leaner beef cuts or substitute with tofu, and choose reduced-sodium sauces to manage both lipid and blood pressure risk factors simultaneously.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; high-heat stir-frying generates oxidised lipids; sodium at 400 mg per 100 g may exacerbate tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with anti-inflammatory proteins such as salmon or tofu, request lower-heat preparation, and reduce sauce quantities to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Egg Fried Noodles (鸡蛋牛肉炒面, Jī Dàn Niú Ròu Chǎo Miàn) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean hawker and restaurant dish consisting of yellow wheat noodles stir-fried at high heat in a wok with thinly sliced beef, scrambled egg, bean sprouts, spring onion, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The dish is characterised by a savoury, slightly smoky wok-hei flavour, a tender-chewy noodle texture, and a rich umami sauce coating. It is commonly served as a single-plate meal in Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops, and Chinese restaurants. Nutritionally, a 450 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 35 g protein, 30 g fat, and 85 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is clinically significant at 400 mg per 100 g (approximately 1800 mg per serving), making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without sauce reduction. The refined carbohydrate load from yellow noodles with minimal dietary fibre (0.89 g per 100 g) creates a medium glycemic index of approximately 50 and a high glycemic load per serving, requiring portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The beef component contributes saturated fat and cholesterol, and its arachidonic acid content is a concern for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or soy allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef and egg fried noodles?
Is fried noodles high in sodium?
What is 鸡蛋牛肉炒面?
Is beef fried noodles suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Singapore stir-fried noodle with meat and egg composite, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries. No single approved-source entry exactly matches this dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for yellow wheat noodles. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match exists in any single approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, mineral panel, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried noodle dish are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific dish category as of 2024. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) corrected to canonical HPB Foodo URL (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/). Input cultural.primary_cuisine field contained a restaurant name ('Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles 福记红烧牛肉面 - Lorong Telok') which is not a cuisine classification; this has been corrected to 'Chinese-Singaporean'. Two duplicate portion entries (500 g and 450 g, both marked is_default=true) were present in the input; the 450 g value is used as the default serving size as the more conservative and commonly cited hawker portion. Trans fat is reported at 0.04 g per 100 g from the input source, consistent with trace levels from high-heat stir-frying of vegetable oil; this is a confirmed low-level value, not zero. All core macros (protein_g 7.78, fat_g 6.67, carbohydrate_g 18.89) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for this dish type. Glycemic index of 50 is considered 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI <= 55 is low).

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values.
Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as a GI of 50 falls into the 'low' category (GI <= 55) according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification. Added a note to the 'notes' field to justify this change.
